results.payload:
1 # Our Security Model vs. SOTA Guardrails — Explain, Refute, Middle Ground 2 3 > Source: /goal task, 2026‑06‑16 — "better explain or refute etc middle ground our security 4 > model thing vs current SOTA e.g. guardrails." Synthesized by Claude (Opus 4.8). 5 > Built on the `fleet/` Fused Agent Kernel cluster (read `INDEX.md` first) and the shipped DOS 6 > substrate under `C:/work`. SOTA calibrated by a 4‑angle web‑research pass, adversarially verified. 7 > **Status:** positioning analysis. It *refutes our own overclaim* as hard as it refutes the 8 > opposing one. A direction‑clarifier, not a commitment. All of `fleet/` is untracked. 9 > 10 > Confidence tags: **[cited]** = primary source fetched · **[measured]** = on‑disk number · 11 > **[est]** = estimate · **[unconf]** = research could not pin to a primary source. 12 13 --- 14 15 ## 0. The one‑line answer 16 17 **Guardrails classify content; our kernel mediates effects.** A guardrail asks *"is this text bad?"* 18 — a classification problem, answered by another model, fundamentally probabilistic and (the literature 19 is blunt) **evadable up to 100%**. Our model asks *"is this action permitted, and did the claimed 20 effect actually happen?"* — a verification problem, answered against evidence the agent did not author, 21 deterministic *within its coverage*. They are **orthogonal layers, not competitors.** 22 23 But there are **two SOTAs**, and conflating them is the trap: 24 - vs. **commercial guardrails** (Llama Guard, Bedrock, OpenAI Moderation, Azure, classic Lakera) — we 25 are a *different, lower layer*; they are content classifiers and we mediate effects. 26 - vs. the **academic security frontier** (CaMeL, FIDES, the Design‑Patterns paper, Willison's dual‑ 27 LLM) — we are **adjacent**, not novel: that camp *already* argues "don't let a model police a 28 model" and builds capability / information‑flow architecture. Our honest delta is one **axis they 29 under‑occupy: effect‑verification — disbelieving the agent's self‑report against a non‑authored 30 witness** (a false "done," a fabricated citation, two agents colliding). 31 32 So the defensible position is **defense‑in‑depth with our kernel as the new bottom layer**, plus one 33 load‑bearing rule the whole frontier converges on: **you cannot build the grounding layer out of more 34 classifiers.** This doc states that case, then **refutes the parts of it that are overclaims.** 35 36 --- 37 38 ## 1. The SOTA is three tiers, not one 39 40 "Guardrails" is not one thing. Calibrating against the real landscape **[cited]**, agent security 41 mid‑2026 stacks in three tiers of increasing rigor — and our model lives below all three: 42 43 | Tier | Examples | Operates on | Nature | 44 |---|---|---|---| 45 | **T1 · Content classifiers** | Llama Guard 4, OpenAI Moderation, Azure Content Safety, AWS Bedrock Guardrails, NeMo input/output rails, classic Lakera | input/output **text** | **probabilistic**, evadable | 46 | **T2 · Deterministic action‑gates** | NeMo *execution* rails, Invariant/Snyk data‑flow rules, LangGraph human‑in‑the‑loop | **tool calls** + data‑flow between tools | mostly **deterministic** policy | 47 | **T3 · Architectural capability / IFC** | **CaMeL** (DeepMind), **FIDES** (MSR), the Design‑Patterns paper, dual‑LLM | the **system** — control/data‑flow, capabilities | **provable by construction** | 48 | **T4 · Effect‑verification (ours)** | DOS: `dos_verify` · `dos_arbitrate` · `dos_citation_resolve` · uninjectable context | the **claim** — did the effect happen? | **deterministic within witness coverage** | 49 50 The crucial observation: **the serious security research has already left T1.** CaMeL's own thesis is 51 *"prompt injection is an architectural problem; you should not rely on a model to police a model"* 52 **[cited, arXiv:2503.18813]**. So when we say "architectural agent security," we are joining a 2025–26 53 frontier, **not** discovering open ground (§5). What that frontier mostly does is **T3 prevention** 54 (stop untrusted data from driving actions). What it mostly does *not* do is **T4 verification** 55 (disbelieve the agent's report of what it did). That gap is our actual contribution. 56 57 --- 58 59 ## 2. The two paradigms, precisely 60 61 | Axis | **Guardrails** (T1, the "SOTA" the prompt names) | **Our model** (T4 over a T2/T3 base) | 62 |---|---|---| 63 | **Object** | *Content* — input/output/retrieved text | *Effect* — the tool‑call syscall + the **claim** about it | 64 | **Core question** | "Is this text bad / unsafe / off‑policy?" | "Is this action permitted, and did the claimed effect happen?" | 65 | **Trust basis** | Another model's / classifier's judgment | Non‑authored evidence (git, file state, exit codes, clock, third‑party reporter) | 66 | **Nature** | **Probabilistic** — FP/FN, evadable | **Deterministic within coverage** — a verdict the agent can't move with words | 67 | **Placement** | *Beside* the model, I/O boundary (ring 3) | *Below* the model, syscall trap (ring 0) | 68 | **Failure mode** | Missed attack (FN) / over‑block (FP); model‑on‑model evasion | **Semantic‑coverage ceiling** / wrongful quarantine | 69 70 The cluster's thesis names the gap directly: 71 72 > **"The model is the untrusted userspace process. DOS is the kernel. The tool call is the trap from 73 > ring 3 to ring 0 — the one boundary where the untrusted process asks the trusted kernel to touch 74 > the world, and the kernel adjudicates from evidence the process did not author."** 75 > — `BRAINSTORM-fused-agent-kernel-2026-06-16.md:84‑86` 76 77 > **"You cannot make the thing you don't trust the kernel. That is why prompt injection is unsolved 78 > everywhere the model is the supervisor."** — `…industry-view-2026-06-16.md:35‑38` 79 80 A T1 guardrail is what you bolt *beside* a model you've decided to trust as supervisor. The kernel is 81 what you build when you decide *not* to. That is the whole disagreement in one sentence — and it is 82 the same sentence CaMeL, FIDES, and Willison are writing in the literature. 83 84 --- 85 86 ## 3. Where guardrails are simply the right tool — do **not** refute these 87 88 Our model has **nothing to say** about a large, real, irreducible class. Conceding it is what makes the 89 rest credible. 90 91 - **Irreducibly‑semantic output policy.** "Is this output toxic / hateful / sexual / self‑harm‑ 92 promoting / leaking PII / off‑topic?" There is **no non‑authored witness for 'is this racist.'** No 93 git commit, no exit code adjudicates it — it is a judgment about content, and a trained classifier 94 is the correct instrument. Llama Guard 4 (12B multimodal, Apr 2025) scores text against the 95 MLCommons S1–S14 hazard taxonomy; Anthropic's **Constitutional Classifiers cut jailbreak success 96 86% → 4.4% at only +0.38% over‑refusal** **[cited, arXiv:2501.18837]** — a genuine, measured win 97 for *content* control. 98 - **Cheap input sanitization.** Catching obvious jailbreak/injection patterns at the boundary before 99 they cost a turn (Meta Prompt Guard 2, Microsoft Prompt Shields, Azure groundedness detection). Even 100 evadable, a cheap probabilistic first filter has positive expected value. 101 - **Groundedness scoring on free text.** When the deliverable *is* prose and there is no structured 102 effect to verify, a groundedness classifier is the only available check. 103 104 **This layer is guardrail territory, permanently.** The kernel does not compete here. 105 106 --- 107 108 ## 4. Where guardrails structurally fail — with the numbers 109 110 These are not "needs a better training set" gaps. They are gaps in the *object* guardrails operate on. 111 112 ### 4.1 Model‑on‑model is circular and demonstrably evadable 113 A guardrail is itself an LLM/classifier policing an LLM. The published evasion record is brutal 114 **[cited]**: 115 - **Spacing out characters** ("I g n o r e …") collapsed Meta Prompt‑Guard‑86M to **0.2% accuracy / 116 99.8% attack success** on 450 harmful prompts (Cisco/Robust Intelligence, 2024). 117 - **Emoji‑smuggling reached 100% evasion against *every one* of six tested guardrails** (Azure Prompt 118 Shield, Meta Prompt Guard, NeMo, Protect AI v1/v2, Vijil) — Hackett et al., **arXiv:2504.11168**; 119 homoglyphs/zero‑width/bidi text similar. 120 - Encoding attacks dropped **Prompt Guard 2** detection to **0.02–0.10** (arXiv:2510.01529). 121 - Even vendor self‑reports concede it: Rebuff's own README — *"cannot provide 100% protection"* 122 (repo archived May 2025); Microsoft — *"Prompt Shields may not catch all attack vectors."* 123 124 The structural reason: a lightweight guard can't decode an obfuscation the downstream LLM happily 125 reconstructs and obeys. Willison's verdict is the load‑bearing line: **"in web application security 126 95% is very much a failing grade"** **[cited, simonwillison.net]** — and these don't reach 95% under 127 adversarial pressure. This is exactly why the cluster leads with the *inversion* rather than a better 128 filter. 129 130 ### 4.2 They inspect content, not effects — so they cannot catch a truthful‑sounding lie 131 A model that emits *"I ran the test suite and all 41 tests pass"* when it ran nothing produces 132 **benign, well‑formed, on‑policy content.** Every content guardrail passes it. The failure is the 133 *correspondence between the claim and the world.* Only an effect‑verifier with the exit‑code/git 134 witness catches it — `dos_verify`'s home turf, **structurally invisible to the entire T1 paradigm.** 135 The research pass reached the same conclusion independently: a false "done" *"passes every content and 136 tool‑permission check; the lie is in the report… catching this needs out‑of‑band effect verification… 137 an architectural witness, not a classifier."* **[cited synthesis]** 138 139 ### 4.3 They have no cross‑agent or fabrication view 140 - **Two agents colliding** on the same files is a *concurrency* event, not a content event — no output 141 classifier on either agent sees it. `dos_arbitrate` decides file‑tree disjointness from partition 142 geometry. 143 - **A fabricated‑but‑plausible citation** (`Wexler v. Cardinal Health, 412 F.3d 220 (4th Cir. 2009)`) 144 is perfectly formatted; a classifier rates it clean. Only resolution against a third‑party reporter 145 (`dos_citation_resolve`) refutes it. 146 147 > These are **integrity / grounding failures**, orthogonal to content‑badness — blind to the guardrail 148 > stack not because it is under‑trained but because they are not in the text. Even T2/T3 (Invariant's 149 > data‑flow rules, CaMeL's IFC) gate the *call*; they do not verify the *report's* truth. 150 151 --- 152 153 ## 5. Where the kernel is the right tool — its irreducible contribution 154 155 The kernel's contribution is the one move guardrails cannot make: **disbelieve the agent and verify its 156 effects from evidence it did not author.** Each DOS verb closes a failure mode no T1 guardrail — and 157 mostly no T3 system — closes: 158 159 | Failure mode (guardrail‑blind) | Kernel mechanism | Evidence it grounds on | 160 |---|---|---| 161 | Model falsely reports an effect ("tests pass") | `dos_verify` / witness‑claim folding | git ancestry, file state, exit codes | 162 | Two workers collide on the same tree | `dos_arbitrate` | file‑tree partition geometry | 163 | Free‑text refusal the model can override | `dos_refuse_reasons` / `dos_check_reason` | closed, typed reason vocabulary | 164 | Fabricated citation / external fact | `dos_citation_resolve` | third‑party reporter | 165 | Poisoned tool result entering context | **uninjectable context** — write‑time admission gate | a verdict the model didn't author | 166 | Context pollution from a bad turn | **KV checkpoint + rollback** | COW snapshot, reversible | 167 168 The clearest differentiator vs guardrails is **uninjectable context** — the prompt‑injection answer 169 that lives *below* the model instead of *beside* it: 170 171 > **"A result does not enter context because the call ran — it passes a write‑time gate, and a 172 > poisoned/failed result is quarantined by a verdict the model didn't author."** 173 > — `BRAINSTORM-fused-agent-kernel-2026-06-16.md:160‑161` 174 175 A guardrail tries to *recognize* the injection in the bytes (recognition — evadable). The context MMU 176 never grants the bytes trusted‑write in the first place (a *capability* problem, not recognition). 177 That categorical difference is shared with CaMeL/FIDES's capability‑and‑IFC design — which is why §6.1 178 is honest that this half is *not* ours alone. 179 180 --- 181 182 ## 6. The refutation — where **our** claim must be honest 183 184 This is the half a partisan writeup omits. The kernel framing is right, but several easy overclaims 185 around it are false, and both the cluster's own record *and* the freshly‑calibrated SOTA say so. 186 187 ### 6.1 The architectural framing is **not novel** — the academic SOTA already lives there 188 This is sharper than "a convergent kernel cluster." The *leading published security research of 189 2025–26* is the architectural/capability/IFC camp **[cited]**: 190 - **CaMeL** (DeepMind, arXiv:2503.18813): dual‑LLM (privileged planner + quarantined parser that 191 can't call tools) + a custom interpreter attaching **capabilities** to every value. **Solves 77% of 192 AgentDojo tasks with *provable* security, vs 84% undefended** — it trades 7 points of utility for a 193 guarantee instead of a probability. 194 - **FIDES** (Microsoft Research, arXiv:2505.23643): formal **information‑flow control**; reports 195 **zero successful policy‑violating injections** on AgentDojo; explicitly argues against "one model 196 policing another." 197 - **"Design Patterns for Securing LLM Agents"** (arXiv:2506.08837, Willison among contributors): 198 *"As long as both agents and their defenses rely on the current class of language models, … it is 199 unlikely that general‑purpose agents can provide meaningful and reliable safety guarantees."* Its 200 six patterns are **all architectural; none is "add a classifier."** 201 202 And the cluster's own audit already said the OS/reference‑monitor framing is crowded: 203 204 > **"0 of 29 scored NOVEL. … A 2025–2026 'agentic LLM serving' + 'agent governance kernel' wave is 205 > independently converging."** — `BRAINSTORM-…2026-06-16.md:507‑509` 206 > **"Microsoft's Agent Governance Toolkit literally calls itself a 'kernel for AI agents.'"** 207 > — `…:132‑134` 208 209 **So "we invented architectural agent security" is false.** The honest delta is narrow and specific: 210 the frontier is overwhelmingly **T3 prevention** (IFC, capabilities, dual‑LLM) against *injection*; 211 our distinctive axis is **T4 effect‑verification** — `dos_verify` / `citation_resolve` / `arbitrate` 212 disbelieving the *self‑report* — which CaMeL/FIDES do not centrally address, and which the research 213 pass independently named as "structurally invisible… needs an architectural witness." That, plus the 214 **shipped** DOS/`headroom` substrate and the *assembly*, is the defensible claim. Not the abstraction. 215 216 ### 6.2 The kernel has its own ceilings — the cluster admits all three 217 - **Semantic‑coverage ceiling.** Effect‑verification only catches what has a non‑authored witness. 218 219 > **"Rungs 0–2 catch syntactic, local, statically‑knowable failures … The ones that burn you are 220 > semantic, remote, stateful … no `--dry-run` catches those."** — `BRAINSTORM-…:190‑192` 221 222 **Pure semantic badness has no witness → the kernel is blind to it → you still need T1 guardrails.** 223 - **Wrongful quarantine is worse than honest noise.** 224 225 > **"A wrongly‑quarantined result is worse than raw pollution. If DOS drops the one stack trace the 226 > model needed, the model proceeds confidently on a sanitized context and fails silently."** 227 > — `COUNTER-fused-agent-kernel-2026-06-16.md:234‑236` 228 229 - **The security/utility tradeoff is fundamental, not engineered away.** AgentDojo (arXiv:2406.13352, 230 NeurIPS 2024) shows defended agents stay partly vulnerable; CaMeL *measures* the cost (77% vs 84%). 231 The same applies to us: confinement strict enough to be safe costs utility. 232 233 ### 6.3 Net: the kernel does not **refute** guardrails — it occupies a **different, lower** layer 234 The refutation cuts both ways. Guardrails don't solve agent security (model‑on‑model, evadable, no 235 effect view). The kernel doesn't solve content policy (no witness for semantics). Anyone selling 236 *either* as the whole answer is wrong — and so is anyone claiming the architectural half is ours alone. 237 238 --- 239 240 ## 7. The middle ground — the synthesis 241 242 Map the tiers onto the rings and they stop competing. They compose *because they fail in different 243 directions.* 244 245 ```mermaid 246 flowchart TB 247 subgraph R3["RING 3 — untrusted model (probabilistic, semantic)"] 248 IN["T1 input guardrails
jailbreak / injection classifier
(cheap first filter, evadable ~100%)"] 249 OUT["T1 output guardrails
toxicity · PII · groundedness
(irreducible semantic policy)"] 250 end 251 subgraph MID["between rings — T2/T3 architectural"] 252 CAP["capability / IFC confinement
CaMeL · FIDES · dual-LLM · data-flow rules
(prevent injection driving actions)"] 253 end 254 subgraph R0["RING 0 — trusted kernel (deterministic, evidential)"] 255 REF["reference monitor on the syscall
allow / deny / transform / require-witness"] 256 VER["T4 effect verification from non-authored evidence
dos_verify · arbitrate · citation_resolve"] 257 MMU["uninjectable context — write-time admission gate
+ KV checkpoint / rollback"] 258 end 259 IN --> MODEL["model turn"] --> OUT 260 MODEL -->|tool call = trap| CAP --> REF --> VER --> MMU 261 MMU -->|admitted bytes only| MODEL 262 ``` 263 264 **Why the layers compose rather than duplicate:** 265 - A T1 **false negative** (a missed injection — up to 100% of the time) is backstopped downstream: 266 the injected instruction is caught when it tries an *effect* the kernel mediates or whose result 267 lacks a witness. 268 - A kernel **coverage gap** (semantic badness with no witness) is backstopped upstream by the content 269 classifier. 270 - Belt‑and‑suspenders that fail in *different directions* is real defense‑in‑depth, not redundancy. 271 272 **The one load‑bearing rule — and the actual defensible thesis:** **you cannot build the effect / 273 grounding layer out of more classifiers.** Stacking another LLM‑judge to verify the first model's 274 claims inherits the same evadability and the same model‑on‑model circularity. The whole frontier says 275 this in chorus — CaMeL ("don't let a model police a model"), the Design‑Patterns paper ("defenses that 276 rely on the current class of language models… unlikely to provide reliable guarantees"), Willison 277 ("95% is a failing grade"). The grounding layer must be **evidential and deterministic**, or it is just 278 a deeper guardrail. **Our specific, under‑occupied slice of that rule is T4: verifying the agent's 279 *effects and claims* against a witness it did not author** — the part even CaMeL/FIDES leave open — and 280 the part the shipped DOS substrate already does. 281 282 --- 283 284 ## 8. How to tell them apart in practice — the blind‑spot table 285 286 The honest version includes the **both‑blind residual zone**, which neither layer closes (AgentDojo's 287 fundamental tradeoff lives there). 288 289 | Attack / failure | Caught by | Why the other layers are blind | 290 |---|---|---| 291 | Toxic / PII output | **T1 content guardrail** | Kernel: no witness for "toxic" | 292 | Jailbreak in user input | **T1 input guardrail** (partial; evadable) | Kernel: not yet an effect | 293 | Injection that drives an *unauthorized* tool call | **T3 capability/IFC** (CaMeL/FIDES) | T1 evadable; kernel sees it only at the syscall | 294 | "I ran the tests, they pass" (false) | **T4 kernel** `dos_verify` | T1/T3: content benign, call authorized | 295 | Two agents collide on files | **T4 kernel** `dos_arbitrate` | T1/T3: not a content or single‑agent event | 296 | Fabricated legal citation | **T4 kernel** `dos_citation_resolve` | T1: well‑formed text; T3: a permitted read | 297 | Poisoned result → destructive action | **T3 + T4** (IFC + admission gate + require‑witness) | T1 evadable | 298 | Poisoned result → *purely informational* nudge that never becomes a checkable effect | **Neither, reliably** | T1 evadable; T3/T4 have no effect/witness to grab — *the residual zone* | 299 300 That last row is the intellectually honest admission: the *fundamental* security/utility tradeoff is 301 not closed by any layer, and we should not claim it is. 302 303 --- 304 305 ## 9. Verdict & dispatch 306 307 **The position to take publicly:** 308 309 > Commercial guardrails operate on **content** and are **probabilistic** model‑on‑model checks — the 310 > literature evades them up to **100%**, and in security 95% is a failing grade. The serious frontier 311 > (CaMeL, FIDES, dual‑LLM) has already moved to **architectural capability / information‑flow** — 312 > *we are part of that camp, not its inventor.* Our distinctive, under‑occupied contribution is the 313 > next axis: **evidential mediation of effects and claims — disbelieving the agent — which cannot be 314 > built from more classifiers** and which our shipped DOS substrate already does. **We add the bottom 315 > ring; we don't remove the top ones, and we don't claim the middle one.** 316 317 **What to refute when we hear it (all three directions):** 318 - "Guardrails solve agent security" → no: model‑on‑model, ~100% evadable, no effect view (§4). 319 - "The kernel makes guardrails obsolete" → no: semantic‑coverage ceiling, no witness for content (§6.2). 320 - "We invented architectural agent security" → no: CaMeL/FIDES/Design‑Patterns own that ground; our 321 novelty is the T4 *verification* axis + the shipped substrate, not the architecture abstraction (§6.1). 322 323 **Dispatch:** 324 - Strategy memo → `dos-private/dispatch-os-trust-kernel-vs-guardrails.md`: lead with the three‑tier 325 landscape (§1) and the "can't build grounding from classifiers, and T4 is the open slice" line (§7). 326 - Subsystem doc → extend `dos-kernel-public/docs/-tool-call-syscall-layer.md` with the §5 327 verb→failure‑mode table and the §8 blind‑spot table as the public "why DOS, not a guardrail" page; 328 cite CaMeL/FIDES as allies, not competitors. 329 - The one demo that proves the wedge: a benign, on‑policy, *false* effect‑claim ("tests pass") that 330 **every content guardrail passes and `dos_verify` catches** — the single clearest separation of 331 classify‑content from verify‑effect (§4.2 / §8 row 4). Pair with a fabricated‑citation catch. 332 333 > **Honest status:** a positioning clarifier over a cluster whose own verdict is 0/29 novel and whose 334 > architectural half is now demonstrably shared with the published SOTA. The contribution here is the 335 > *framing* — three‑tier landscape, content‑vs‑effect, the can't‑classify‑your‑way‑to‑grounding rule, 336 > the T4 effect‑verification delta, and the blind‑spot table — not a new primitive. Where it's wrong: 337 > §8's residual zone is real and unclosed; do not oversell the middle ground as total coverage. 338 339 --- 340 341 ### Sources (verified primary) 342 Llama Guard 4 model card (Apr 2025, S1–S14; English output recall 69%/FPR 11%) · Constitutional 343 Classifiers arXiv:2501.18837 (86%→4.4%, +0.38% over‑refusal, +23.7% compute) · NeMo Guardrails 344 arXiv:2310.10501 (five rail types incl. execution) · Bypassing LLM Guardrails arXiv:2504.11168 345 (emoji‑smuggling 100% evasion, all six guards) · Cisco Prompt‑Guard‑86M spacing bypass (99.8% ASR) · 346 Controlled‑Release Prompting arXiv:2510.01529 (PG2 → 0.02–0.10) · **CaMeL** arXiv:2503.18813 (77% vs 347 84% AgentDojo, provable) · **FIDES** arXiv:2505.23643 (IFC, zero injections) · **Design Patterns** 348 arXiv:2506.08837 · AgentDojo arXiv:2406.13352 (NeurIPS 2024) · Invariant Labs / Snyk (acq. Jun 2025, 349 data‑flow rules) · LangGraph human‑in‑the‑loop (deterministic interrupt) · AWS Bedrock Guardrails 350 (text‑only) · Simon Willison: lethal trifecta (16 Jun 2025), "95% is a failing grade," dual‑LLM 351 pattern. **Flagged [unconf]:** headline FP/FN for NeMo/Guardrails‑AI/OpenAI‑Mod/Azure; published ASR 352 vs Lakera Guard; Lakera's 2025 acquirer (Cisco vs Check Point conflict). 353

results.payload:
1	// Package bench is the A/B ablation runner behind `fak bench`. It replays a
2	// FROZEN tool-call trace through the one kernel twice — once with the vDSO on,
3	// once off — timing the in-process adjudication boundary (Submit) per call, and
4	// measures the spawned-hook baseline by spawning `fak hook` once per decide. The
5	// delta between in-process and spawned is the fusion win, measured on THIS
6	// machine (not cited), apples-to-apples: the same decide logic, two transports.
7	//
8	// The headline KPI is call-mix-independent and cannot go red (unit 82): an
9	// in-process function fold is unconditionally cheaper than a process spawn. The
10	// vDSO token delta is reported as a soft secondary (unit 83), never the gate.
11	//
12	// os/exec lives HERE (the baseline harness), never on the dispatch hot path —
13	// the absence proof (unit 72) targets internal/kernel, not internal/bench.
14	package bench
15	
16	import (
17		"bytes"
18		"context"
19		"crypto/sha256"
20		"encoding/hex"
21		"encoding/json"
22		"os"
23		"os/exec"
24		"runtime"
25		"sort"
26		"strconv"
27		"time"
28	
29		"github.com/anthony-chaudhary/fak/internal/abi"
30		"github.com/anthony-chaudhary/fak/internal/kernel"
31		"github.com/anthony-chaudhary/fak/internal/metrics"
32	)
33	
34	// Call is one trace entry.
35	type Call struct {
36		Tool string            `json:"tool"`
37		Args json.RawMessage   `json:"args"`
38		Meta map[string]string `json:"meta,omitempty"`
39	}
40	
41	// Trace is a frozen, replayable tool-call slice.
42	type Trace struct {
43		SliceID string `json:"slice_id"`
44		Calls   []Call `json:"calls"`
45	}
46	
47	// LoadTrace reads a trace JSON file.
48	func LoadTrace(path string) (*Trace, error) {
49		b, err := os.ReadFile(path)
50		if err != nil {
51			return nil, err
52		}
53		var t Trace
54		if err := json.Unmarshal(b, &t); err != nil {
55			return nil, err
56		}
57		return &t, nil
58	}
59	
60	// WorkloadHash is a stable hash of the trace's calls (the identical-workload
61	// guard input, unit 80/81). Independent of arm.
62	func (t *Trace) WorkloadHash() string {
63		h := sha256.New()
64		h.Write([]byte(t.SliceID))
65		// sort-independent: calls are replayed in order, so hash them in order.
66		for _, c := range t.Calls {
67			h.Write([]byte(c.Tool))
68			h.Write([]byte{0})
69			h.Write(c.Args)
70			h.Write([]byte{0})
71			keys := make([]string, 0, len(c.Meta))
72			for k := range c.Meta {
73				keys = append(keys, k)
74			}
75			sort.Strings(keys)
76			for _, k := range keys {
77				h.Write([]byte(k + "=" + c.Meta[k] + ";"))
78			}
79		}
80		return hex.EncodeToString(h.Sum(nil))[:16]
81	}
82	
83	// adjReps is the inner calibration loop for the adjudication-latency measurement.
84	// The in-process fold is faster than the OS clock granularity (~100ns-1µs on
85	// Windows), so we time a batch of identical decides and divide — the standard
86	// microbenchmark technique — to recover a real sub-microsecond per-call number.
87	const adjReps = 2000
88	
89	// RunArm replays the trace through a fresh kernel with the vDSO on/off and
90	// returns the arm's metrics. The headline (adjudication p50) is the per-call cost
91	// of the in-process Decide fold — the SAME logic the spawned `fak hook` baseline
92	// runs, so the two are apples-to-apples. Submit/Reap are still driven for the
93	// counters, token accounting, and MMU admission.
94	func RunArm(ctx context.Context, t *Trace, engineID string, vdsoOn bool, label string) (metrics.Arm, error) {
95		k := kernel.New(engineID)
96		k.SetVDSO(vdsoOn)
97		res := abi.ActiveResolver()
98		var h metrics.Hist
99		arm := metrics.Arm{Label: label, Calls: len(t.Calls)}
100		for _, c := range t.Calls {
101			args := []byte(c.Args)
102			if len(args) == 0 {
103				args = []byte("{}")
104			}
105			ref, err := res.Put(ctx, args)
106			if err != nil {
107				return arm, err
108			}
109			tc := &abi.ToolCall{Tool: c.Tool, Args: ref, Meta: c.Meta}
110	
111			// Measure the in-process adjudication boundary with an inner calibration
112			// loop (clock-granularity-robust).
113			t0 := time.Now()
114			for i := 0; i < adjReps; i++ {
115				_ = k.Decide(ctx, tc)
116			}
117			h.RecordNs(int64(time.Since(t0)) / adjReps)
118	
119			// Drive the functional path once for counters / tokens / admission.
120			hdl, _ := k.Submit(ctx, tc)
121			r, err := k.Reap(ctx, hdl)
122			if err != nil {
123				continue
124			}
125			in, out := tokens(r)
126			arm.InTokens += in
127			arm.OutTokens += out
128		}
129		cc := k.Counters()
130		arm.EngineCalls = cc.EngineCalls
131		arm.VDSOHits = cc.VDSOHits
132		arm.Denies = cc.Denies
133		arm.Quarantines = cc.Quarantines
134		arm.P50Ns = h.P50()
135		arm.P99Ns = h.P99()
136		arm.MeanNs = h.Mean()
137		arm.Buckets = h.Buckets()
138		return arm, nil
139	}
140	
141	func tokens(r *abi.Result) (in, out int64) {
142		if r == nil || r.Meta == nil {
143			return 0, 0
144		}
145		in, _ = strconv.ParseInt(r.Meta["input_tokens"], 10, 64)
146		out, _ = strconv.ParseInt(r.Meta["output_tokens"], 10, 64)
147		return in, out
148	}
149	
150	// MeasureSpawnedBaseline spawns `binPath hook` once per sample, piping a call on
151	// stdin, and times the round-trip — the honest, same-machine spawned-hook floor
152	// (unit 23). Each spawn runs the SAME decide logic the in-process path runs.
153	func MeasureSpawnedBaseline(binPath string, sample Call, n int) (metrics.Baseline, error) {
154		payload, _ := json.Marshal(sample)
155		var h metrics.Hist
156		for i := 0; i < n; i++ {
157			t0 := time.Now()
158			cmd := exec.Command(binPath, "hook")
159			cmd.Stdin = bytes.NewReader(payload)
160			var out bytes.Buffer
161			cmd.Stdout = &out
162			if err := cmd.Run(); err != nil {
163				return metrics.Baseline{}, err
164			}
165			h.Record(time.Since(t0))
166		}
167		return metrics.Baseline{
168			Source:     "spawned `fak hook` per decide, this machine",
169			P50Ns:      h.P50(),
170			P99Ns:      h.P99(),
171			Calls:      n,
172			SpawnModel: "process-per-decide (" + runtime.GOOS + ")",
173		}, nil
174	}
175	
176	// Options configure a full A/B run.
177	type Options struct {
178		EngineID       string
179		EngineModel    string
180		BinPath        string // path to the fak binary for the spawned baseline ("" => skip, RED)
181		BaselineN      int
182		LiveTranscript string // a real-engine transcript hash, or "" => live_seam_unverified
183	}
184	
185	// Run executes both arms + the baseline and assembles the report. It enforces the
186	// identical-workload guard (unit 81) before comparing.
187	func Run(ctx context.Context, t *Trace, opt Options) (*metrics.Report, error) {
188		on, err := RunArm(ctx, t, opt.EngineID, true, "vdso_on")
189		if err != nil {
190			return nil, err
191		}
192		off, err := RunArm(ctx, t, opt.EngineID, false, "vdso_off")
193		if err != nil {
194			return nil, err
195		}
196	
197		rep := &metrics.Report{
198			Provenance: metrics.Provenance{
199				Command:      "fak bench --suite " + t.SliceID,
200				EngineModel:  opt.EngineModel,
201				SliceID:      t.SliceID,
202				WorkloadHash: t.WorkloadHash(),
203				GoVersion:    runtime.Version(),
204				OS:           runtime.GOOS,
205				GeneratedBy:  "fak/internal/bench",
206			},
207			On:  on,
208			Off: off,
209		}
210	
211		// Identical-workload guard: both arms replay the same trace, so the hashes
212		// match by construction — but we assert it (unit 81).
213		if err := rep.Validate(t.WorkloadHash(), t.WorkloadHash()); err != nil {
214			return nil, err
215		}
216	
217		// Spawned-hook baseline.
218		if opt.BinPath != "" {
219			n := opt.BaselineN
220			if n <= 0 {
221				n = 30
222			}
223			sample := Call{Tool: "get_reservation_details", Args: json.RawMessage(`{"id":"ABC123"}`),
224				Meta: map[string]string{"readOnlyHint": "true", "idempotentHint": "true"}}
225			base, err := MeasureSpawnedBaseline(opt.BinPath, sample, n)
226			if err != nil {
227				return nil, err
228			}
229			rep.Baseline = base
230		}
231	
232		// KPIs.
233		hitRate := 0.0
234		if on.Calls > 0 {
235			hitRate = float64(on.VDSOHits) / float64(on.Calls)
236		}
237		rep.KPIs = metrics.KPIs{
238			ToolCallP50Ns:        on.P50Ns,
239			ToolCallP99Ns:        on.P99Ns,
240			VDSOHitRate:          hitRate,
241			ContextPollutionRate: rate(on.Quarantines, int64(on.Calls)),
242			TokensPerTask:        float64(on.InTokens+on.OutTokens) / float64(max1(on.Calls)),
243		}
244	
245		// Secondary soft token delta (unit 83): on vs off (never gates).
246		offTok := off.InTokens + off.OutTokens
247		onTok := on.InTokens + on.OutTokens
248		if offTok > 0 {
249			rep.TokenDeltaPct = 100 * float64(offTok-onTok) / float64(offTok)
250		}
251		// tokencost (unit 84): a representative $/Mtok blended rate applied to on-arm.
252		rep.DollarPerTask = dollar(onTok, on.Calls)
253	
254		rep.ComputeGate()
255	
256		if opt.LiveTranscript != "" {
257			rep.LiveSeam = opt.LiveTranscript
258		} else {
259			rep.LiveSeam = "live_seam_unverified" // honest RED flag (unit 46)
260		}
261		return rep, nil
262	}
263	
264	func rate(a, b int64) float64 {
265		if b == 0 {
266			return 0
267		}
268		return float64(a) / float64(b)
269	}
270	func max1(n int) int {
271		if n < 1 {
272			return 1
273		}
274		return n
275	}
276	
277	// dollar applies a blended $3/Mtok-in, $15/Mtok-out representative rate (a stand-in
278	// for the tokencost 218-model table; the figure is illustrative, not billed).
279	func dollar(totalTok int64, calls int) float64 {
280		// Without the in/out split here we approximate at the blended input rate.
281		return float64(totalTok) / 1e6 * 3.0
282	}

1	# CLAIMS.md — the fak v0.1 honesty ledger
2	
3	Every capability claim carries **exactly one** tag:
4	
5	- `[SHIPPED]` — real code on the critical path, closed by a mechanical witness (a `go test`, a `go build`, a benchmark field, a file read-back). Reproducible now.
6	- `[SIMULATED]` — modeled with labeled stand-in data (no GPU / no live engine on the build box); the seam is real, the numbers are illustrative.
7	- `[STUB]` — plumbing present, behavior deferred; clearly labeled, returns a STUB/no-op result.
8	
9	The lint witness (unit 96): every line beginning with `- [` carries one and only one of the three tags.
10	
11	## The product
12	
13	- [SHIPPED] One statically-linked Go binary (`fak`) runs an agentic tool loop where every tool call crosses one in-process syscall boundary. Witness: `go build ./...` exit 0; `fak run --trace ...` completes.
14	- [SHIPPED] Process-level fusion: harness + reference monitor + vDSO + pre-flight + context-MMU collapsed into one Go address space; no spawned hook, no IPC on the decide path. Witness: `TestNoOsExecOnHotPath` (ABSENCE proof, unit 72).
15	- [SHIPPED] The frozen ABI is a machine-checked contract (additive-only). Witness: `TestABIGoldenFreeze` over `internal/abi/testdata/abi_v0.1.golden` (unit 2/9).
16	
17	## The headline KPI (cannot go red — unit 82)
18	
19	- [SHIPPED] In-process adjudication p50 vs a spawned-hook baseline, measured on THIS machine, apples-to-apples (same `Fold` decide, two transports). Measured: ~1.3 µs in-process vs ~6–8 ms spawned `fak hook` ⇒ ~5,000× (full-binary spawn). Witness: `report.json` `gate_primary=="pass"`; `baseline.json` `p50_ns > 1ms`.
20	- [SHIPPED] The gate is call-mix-independent: it times the adjudication fold, not the tool mix, so a low vDSO hit-rate cannot turn it red.
21	
22	## Adjudication (the in-process DOS reference monitor)
23	
24	- [SHIPPED] Provable refusal ⇒ `Deny`, unprovable ⇒ `Defer` (mirrors dos-preflake `decide.go`); default-deny on empty policy. Witness: `TestFoldDefaultDenyEmptyPolicy` (unit 15).
25	- [SHIPPED] Structured refusal from a closed 12-reason vocabulary + a bounded-disclosure witness (SELF_MODIFY returns only the offending glob). Witness: adjudicator tests (units 19, 20). Prior art: DOS `dos_refuse_reasons`; SMT unsat-core.
26	- [SHIPPED] Deny-as-value: a refusal carries a derived disposition (RETRYABLE/WAIT/ESCALATE/TERMINAL) the loop consumes (unit 74). Prior art: eBPF verdict, deny-loopback design.
27	- [SHIPPED] Batch adjudication (set shape) equals serial, in one pass (unit 75). Prior art: `dos-plan-price` generalized; speculative-decoding inverted.
28	
29	## Tool vDSO (3-tier local fast path)
30	
31	- [SHIPPED] Tier-1 pure registry (gated on readOnlyHint+idempotentHint, re-checked not trusted), tier-2 content-addressed cache (world-versioned, LRU), tier-3 static table. Witness: `vdso` tests (units 25–38). Prior art: kernel vDSO; RadixAttention prefix reuse.
32	- [SHIPPED] Arg-order-independent content keys (canonicalized JSON). Witness: vdso canonicalization test (unit 26).
33	- [SHIPPED] A write-shaped completion bumps the world-version and invalidates the cache (soundness: a hit equals a fresh call). Witness: units 28, 38.
34	- [SIMULATED] Real-world vDSO hit-rate: the demo trace `tau2-smoke` is deliberately cache-favorable (~50% hits). The EXPERIMENTS measured addressable purity on real tau2-airline is ~0.7% — far below a useful threshold. The vDSO is therefore an UPSIDE secondary, never the headline. Witness: `report.json` `vdso_hit_rate` (reported, never gated, unit 33/83).
35	
36	## Pre-flight ladder + grammar rung
37	
38	- [SHIPPED] Rung-0 static parse + rung-1 JSON-Schema validation, cheapest-first, escalate-on-pass; hard-negative label harvesting. Witness: `preflight` tests (units 47–51).
39	- [SHIPPED] Grammar rung: positional→named auto-repair (in-syscall TRANSFORM, no model turn) for arity-matched calls; unrepairable ⇒ Deny(MISROUTE); fail-open on unknown grammar; content-addressed grammar dedup. Witness: `grammar` tests (units 52–57). Prior art: GBNF/XGrammar; the tool-invocation-grammar design.
40	- [STUB] Rung-2 dry-run probe and rung-3 sandbox probe are plumbed and labeled STUB (units 56, 59).
41	- [STUB] Decode-time logit-mask (grammar-constrained generation) — the strongest form (never-emit a malformed call) — requires owning the decode loop; not in v0.1.
42	
43	## Context-MMU (write-time result admission)
44	
45	- [SHIPPED] Result-admit gate: secret-shaped and prompt-injection/poison results are QUARANTINED (held out of context, paged to a stub pointer); oversize benign results page out to a <2KB pointer (TRANSFORM); byte-repeat pollution quarantined. Witness: `ctxmmu` tests (units 61–70), `testdata/poison.json` fixture (unit 68).
46	- [SHIPPED] Page-in is gated on an explicit witness `Clear()` (unit 67); pollution-rate counter (unit 66); shared content-addressed blob store with the vDSO (unit 64).
47	- [SIMULATED] `headroom` (Rust) page-out codec: the v0.1 default is pure-Go content-addressed page-out; the headroom backend is an optional labeled seam, not on the critical path (unit 69).
48	
49	## Engine
50	
51	- [SHIPPED] OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions` client, base_url-swappable local↔remote, bounded timeout + backoff; cassette record/replay for deterministic offline runs; token-usage extraction; a deterministic mock engine. Witness: `engine` tests (units 39–45).
52	- [SIMULATED] metrics-service scrape adapter / KV-residency / token-per-watt: labeled SIMULATED telemetry — there is no GPU or watt source on the build box (units 43, 78).
53	- [SHIPPED] Live-seam honesty: a run carries a real transcript hash XOR the explicit RED flag `live_seam_unverified` — never a silent skip (unit 46).
54	- [SHIPPED] The live seam is now exercised end-to-end: `fak agent` drove this kernel with a real OpenAI-compatible model (gemini-2.5-flash / -flash-lite and local Qwen2.5-1.5B/0.5B), every run carrying a real `transcript_sha` (not the `live_seam_unverified` flag). The v0.1 `fak bench` remains a deterministic OFFLINE replay by design; the live A/B is the separate `fak agent` lane. Witness: `experiments/agent-live/*.json` (each `live:true`); see `LIVE-RESULTS.md`.
55	
56	## Stewards + RSI ship-gate
57	
58	- [SHIPPED] Single-invariant stewards (secret-in-context, lease-disjointness, kpi-regression, vdso-soundness) that fire only with an independently-authored witness; a meta-steward prunes never-firing stewards. Witness: `steward` tests (units 87–92).
59	- [SHIPPED] RSI-as-ship-gate: keep-or-revert on a non-forgeable keep-bit (strict metric gain AND suite-green AND truth-clean), applied in an isolated git worktree; a run of non-keeps trips an escalation breaker. Witness: `shipgate` tests (units 93–95).
60	
61	## What v0.1 is NOT
62	
63	- [STUB] No zero-copy KV co-residence with the engine (vLLM owns the KV cache in Python/CUDA; ~120h, out of scope). The `Ref`/`Resolver`/`RegionBackend` seam is frozen so it is a backend swap later, but v0.1 is a copy-CAS.
64	- [STUB] No fine-tuned syscall model and no AsyncLM interrupt behavior — the typed `LabelRow`/`VerdictKind` training targets exist, the model does not.
65	- [SIMULATED] No GPU dependency; continuous-batching / token-per-watt are read-only SIMULATED telemetry.
66	
67	## Prior-art posture
68	
69	- [SHIPPED] Consistent with the cluster's 0/29-NOVEL finding: every primitive here is established/emerging; the contribution is the ASSEMBLY (a fused, fail-open, witness-gated kernel with the tool call promoted to a syscall), not any single mechanism.

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""
3	session_audit.py — reusable auditor for Claude Code session-transcript JSONL files.
4	
5	The transcripts live under <claude-home>/projects/<namespace>/<session-uuid>.jsonl and
6	carry EXACT token accounting (the `.dos/` tool-stream telemetry only has content digests,
7	no token counts — so this is the tool that can answer the token-weighted questions:
8	real input:output ratio, real prompt-cache / KV reuse, real cost, real tool mix).
9	
10	Schema (per JSONL line, one record):
11	  type=assistant : message.usage = {input_tokens, output_tokens,
12	                     cache_read_input_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens,
13	                     server_tool_use{web_search_requests,web_fetch_requests}, iterations[...]}
14	                   message.content = [ {type: thinking|text|tool_use}, ... ]
15	  type=user      : message.content = str (typed prompt / slash-command wrapper / hook text)
16	                                   or [ {type: tool_result, content: ...}, ... ]
17	  plus meta records: last-prompt, mode, permission-mode, attachment, file-history-snapshot,
18	                     system, queue-operation, ai-title, summary
19	
20	Usage:
21	  python session_audit.py discover [--since-days N] [--root DIR ...]
22	  python session_audit.py audit  [--since-days N] [--root DIR ...] [--json OUT] [--md OUT]
23	  python session_audit.py deep   <session.jsonl>          # follow ONE trajectory top-to-bottom
24	
25	All numbers from token usage are EXACT. Cost uses the PRICING table below (an ASSUMPTION,
26	clearly flagged) — edit it to match current rates; token counts are the ground truth.
27	"""
28	import sys, os, json, glob, argparse, statistics, collections, datetime

36	    "fable":  ( 3.0,  3.75, 0.30, 15.0),
37	    "_default": (15.0, 18.75, 1.50, 75.0),
38	}
39	PRICING_IS_ASSUMPTION = True
40	
41	DEFAULT_ROOTS = [
42	    os.path.expanduser("~/.claude-agent/projects"),
43	    os.path.expanduser("~/.claude/projects"),
44	]
45	# transient test-fixture / temp-workspace namespaces — never "our own sessions"
46	EXCLUDE_NS_SUBSTR = ["pytest-of-USER", "AppData-Local-Temp", "workspace", "-ws", "test_"]
47	NS_INCLUDE_PREFIX = "C--work"   # real project namespaces; override with --ns-prefix '' / --all
48	
49	READ_ONLY_TOOLS = {"Read", "Glob", "Grep", "LS", "NotebookRead", "WebFetch", "WebSearch",
50	                   "TodoRead", "ToolSearch"}
51	# Bash/Edit/Write/NotebookEdit/Task/Workflow/etc. are side-effecting or spawn.
52	
53	def price_for(model):
54	    m = (model or "").lower()
55	    for key, rates in PRICING.items():

results.payload:
1	// Package preflight is the pre-flight rung ladder: cheapest-first
2	// well-formedness checks that catch a malformed/unsafe call BEFORE it fires, so a
3	// dead branch never spawns a process or burns a model turn. Each rung is run in
4	// order and escalation only happens on a pass (unit 49). A catch is recorded as a
5	// typed hard-negative (passed cheap rung k, failed expensive rung k+1) — the
6	// self-labeling signal that trains the syscall-tuned model (unit 50).
7	//
8	// Rungs in v0.1:
9	//
10	//	rung 0  static parse  — are the args even valid JSON? (unit 47)
11	//	rung 1  schema check  — required fields present + types match a JSON Schema
12	//	                        (unit 48)
13	//
14	// It registers as a low-rank Adjudicator (runs before the authoritative monitor);
15	// the kernel's fold takes the most-restrictive verdict, so a rung Deny wins over
16	// a later Allow. A well-formed call returns Defer (the rung has nothing to prove).
17	package preflight
18	
19	import (
20		"context"
21		"encoding/json"
22		"sync"
23	
24		"github.com/anthony-chaudhary/fak/internal/abi"
25	)
26	
27	// FieldType is a minimal JSON Schema scalar type.
28	type FieldType string
29	
30	const (
31		TypeString FieldType = "string"
32		TypeNumber FieldType = "number"
33		TypeBool   FieldType = "boolean"
34		TypeObject FieldType = "object"
35		TypeArray  FieldType = "array"
36		TypeAny    FieldType = ""
37	)
38	
39	// Schema is a tiny JSON-Schema subset: required keys + their expected types.
40	type Schema struct {
41		Required map[string]FieldType
42	}
43	
44	// Ladder is the rung ladder. Construct with New; Default is registered.
45	type Ladder struct {
46		mu        sync.RWMutex
47		schemas   map[string]Schema
48		total     int64
49		caught    int64
50		negatives [][]byte // labeled hard-negative JSONL rows (unit 50)
51	}
52	
53	func New() *Ladder { return &Ladder{schemas: map[string]Schema{}} }
54	
55	// SetSchema installs a schema for a tool (rung-1 input).
56	func (l *Ladder) SetSchema(tool string, s Schema) {
57		l.mu.Lock()
58		l.schemas[tool] = s
59		l.mu.Unlock()
60	}
61	
62	func (l *Ladder) Caps() []abi.Capability { return nil }
63	
64	// Adjudicate runs the rungs cheapest-first.
65	func (l *Ladder) Adjudicate(ctx context.Context, c *abi.ToolCall) abi.Verdict {
66		l.mu.Lock()
67		l.total++
68		l.mu.Unlock()
69	
70		args := refBytes(ctx, c.Args)
71	
72		// rung 0: static parse. Empty args is well-formed (no body). Non-empty must
73		// parse as a JSON object.
74		if len(args) > 0 {
75			var probe map[string]any
76			if err := json.Unmarshal(args, &probe); err != nil {
77				return l.caughtAt(c, -1, 0, abi.ReasonMalformed)
78			}
79		}
80	
81		// rung 1: schema validation (only if a schema is known; else escalate-by-defer).
82		l.mu.RLock()
83		s, known := l.schemas[c.Tool]
84		l.mu.RUnlock()
85		if known && len(s.Required) > 0 {
86			var m map[string]any
87			_ = json.Unmarshal(args, &m)
88			if m == nil {
89				m = map[string]any{}
90			}
91			for k, ty := range s.Required {
92				v, present := m[k]
93				if !present {
94					return l.caughtAt(c, 0, 1, abi.ReasonMalformed)
95				}
96				if !typeOK(v, ty) {
97					return l.caughtAt(c, 0, 1, abi.ReasonMalformed)
98				}
99			}
100		}
101	
102		// passed every rung: the ladder has nothing to prove, defer to the monitor.
103		return abi.Verdict{Kind: abi.VerdictDefer, By: "preflight"}
104	}
105	
106	func (l *Ladder) caughtAt(c *abi.ToolCall, passed, failed int, r abi.ReasonCode) abi.Verdict {
107		row := abi.LabelRow{
108			CallHash:   callHash(c),
109			RungPassed: passed,
110			RungFailed: failed,
111			Verdict:    abi.VerdictDeny,
112			Reason:     r,
113		}
114		b, _ := json.Marshal(struct {
115			CallHash   string `json:"call_hash"`
116			RungPassed int    `json:"rung_passed"`
117			RungFailed int    `json:"rung_failed"`
118			Verdict    string `json:"verdict"`
119			Reason     string `json:"reason"`
120		}{row.CallHash, row.RungPassed, row.RungFailed, "deny", abi.ReasonName(r)})
121		l.mu.Lock()
122		l.caught++
123		l.negatives = append(l.negatives, b)
124		l.mu.Unlock()
125		return abi.Verdict{Kind: abi.VerdictDeny, Reason: r, By: "preflight"}
126	}
127	
128	// CatchRate returns caught/total (unit 51).
129	func (l *Ladder) CatchRate() (caught, total int64, rate float64) {
130		l.mu.RLock()
131		defer l.mu.RUnlock()
132		if l.total > 0 {
133			rate = float64(l.caught) / float64(l.total)
134		}
135		return l.caught, l.total, rate
136	}
137	
138	// Negatives returns the labeled hard-negative JSONL rows harvested so far.
139	func (l *Ladder) Negatives() [][]byte {
140		l.mu.RLock()
141		defer l.mu.RUnlock()
142		return append([][]byte(nil), l.negatives...)
143	}
144	
145	func typeOK(v any, ty FieldType) bool {
146		switch ty {
147		case TypeAny:
148			return true
149		case TypeString:
150			_, ok := v.(string)
151			return ok
152		case TypeNumber:
153			_, ok := v.(float64)
154			return ok
155		case TypeBool:
156			_, ok := v.(bool)
157			return ok
158		case TypeObject:
159			_, ok := v.(map[string]any)
160			return ok
161		case TypeArray:
162			_, ok := v.([]any)
163			return ok
164		}
165		return true
166	}
167	
168	func refBytes(ctx context.Context, r abi.Ref) []byte {
169		if r.Kind == abi.RefInline {
170			return r.Inline
171		}
172		if res := abi.ActiveResolver(); res != nil {
173			if b, err := res.Resolve(ctx, r); err == nil {
174				return b
175			}
176		}
177		return nil
178	}
179	
180	func callHash(c *abi.ToolCall) string {
181		return c.Tool + ":" + c.Args.Digest
182	}
183	
184	// Default is the registered ladder.
185	var Default = New()
186	
187	func init() {
188		abi.RegisterAdjudicator(10, Default) // before the rank-100 monitor
189		abi.RegisterCapability("preflight.v1")
190	}
191

results.payload:
1	// Package abi is the FROZEN wave-0 spine of fak (the Fused Agent Kernel).
2	//
3	// This is the ONE file every fleet worker imports and the ONE tree no worker may
4	// lease (dos-arbitrate denies a lease on internal/abi; dos-plan-price refuses any
5	// partition that collides with it). Once frozen it is ADDITIVE-ONLY: fields are
6	// only ever ADDED with zero-value defaults, never removed or repurposed. A golden
7	// conformance test (testdata/abi_v0.1.golden) fails any breaking change, turning
8	// the freeze into a machine-checked contract.
9	//
10	// DESIGN STANCE: a Linux-syscall-table-shaped stable spine. No subsystem name
11	// (AGT, DOS, vDSO, rung, steward, headroom, litellm) ever appears here. Every
12	// v0.1 subsystem AND every future idea attaches as a registered driver in its own
13	// package via the registries in registry.go — a new idea is a new package + one
14	// register() call + (optionally) one additive envelope field guarded by a
15	// Capability, NEVER an edit to this file.
16	//
17	// Three real precedents the openness is modeled on:
18	//   - Linux syscall table : stable numbered ops, never renumbered, append-only.
19	//   - io_uring opcode table: an unknown opcode returns -EINVAL, it does not crash.
20	//   - LSM stackable hooks  : new hooks register; the core walker is untouched.
21	//
22	// The FOUR seams below are the ones that, if missed at freeze time, force a
23	// fleet-wide recompile later. They are all present now, defaulted so v0.1 ignores
24	// them: (1) Verdict is an additive discriminated union, (2) payloads are
25	// addressable Refs not copied bytes, (3) sync Syscall is defined OVER async
26	// Submit/Reap, (4) a provisional speculation/txn lifecycle rides the envelope.
27	package abi
28	
29	import "context"
30	
31	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
32	// ABI VERSION + CAPABILITY NEGOTIATION (the ELF header)
33	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
34	
35	// ABIMajor never bumps in v0 (a bump is a breaking change, which the golden test
36	// forbids). ABIMinor increments only for additive surface (a new reserved range,
37	// a new core-blessed capability).
38	const (
39		ABIMajor = 0
40		ABIMinor = 1
41	)
42	
43	// Capability is a negotiated feature token (OPEN set). A driver advertises the
44	// caps it needs; Kernel.Negotiate intersects advertised-vs-registered, so a
45	// worker built before a capability existed never sees it and degrades to the
46	// conservative default. Examples: "async", "speculative", "zerocopy",
47	// "witness.v2", "trust.federated", "share.fleet".
48	type Capability string
49	
50	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
51	// SEAM 2 — ADDRESSABLE PAYLOAD (the zero-copy + taint + share seam)
52	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
53	
54	// Ref is an ADDRESSABLE handle to bytes, never the bytes themselves. v0.1 backs
55	// it with a content-addressed blob store (a copy); a later in-address-space impl
56	// (co-residing tool args/results with the KV cache — brainstorm 3.1a) is a
57	// Resolver/RegionBackend swap behind Capability "zerocopy". Callers only ever see
58	// Ref + Resolver, so the wire shape never changes.
59	//
60	// Ref also carries the provenance fields the cross-agent shared-result pool needs
61	// (Taint, Scope): a result is never shared more widely than its scope, and
62	// sharing a result shares its taint. Defaults (Tainted, ScopeAgent) mean "never
63	// shared, untrusted" — the fail-closed baseline.
64	type Ref struct {
65		Kind   RefKind    // Inline | Blob | Region
66		Digest string     // content hash: stable identity for the vDSO + provenance
67		Inline []byte     // populated iff Kind==RefInline (small payloads)
68		Handle uint64     // backend-opaque (CAS key / arena offset); meaningless w/o Resolver
69		Len    int64      // payload length
70		Taint  TaintLabel // Trusted | Tainted | Quarantined (default Tainted)
71		Scope  ShareScope // Agent | Fleet | Tenant (default ScopeAgent)
72	}
73	
74	type RefKind uint8
75	
76	const (
77		RefInline RefKind = iota // bytes embedded in Inline
78		RefBlob                  // bytes live in a content-addressed store (Handle = key)
79		RefRegion                // bytes live in an addressable region (Handle = addr/offset)
80	)
81	
82	// TaintLabel is a CLOSED, additive lattice (trusted < tainted < quarantined).
83	type TaintLabel uint8
84	
85	const (
86		TaintTainted     TaintLabel = iota // default: untrusted unless proven
87		TaintTrusted                       // adjudicated trusted; shareable per Scope
88		TaintQuarantined                   // held out of context by the MMU
89	)
90	
91	// ShareScope is a CLOSED, additive isolation scope. Default ScopeAgent = private.
92	type ShareScope uint8
93	
94	const (
95		ScopeAgent  ShareScope = iota // private to one agent (fail-closed default)
96		ScopeFleet                    // shareable across the fleet's trusted partition
97		ScopeTenant                   // shareable within a tenant boundary
98	)
99	
100	// Resolver is the OPEN backend behind every Ref. Swapping it (copy-CAS ->
101	// shared-arena zero-copy) is a backend change, not an ABI change. Registered via
102	// RegisterRegionBackend; the MMU's blob store is the v0.1 default.
103	type Resolver interface {
104		Resolve(ctx context.Context, r Ref) ([]byte, error) // materialize on demand
105		Put(ctx context.Context, b []byte) (Ref, error)
106	}
107	
108	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
109	// SEAM 4 — PROVISIONAL LIFECYCLE (the speculative + transactional-context seam)
110	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
111	
112	// SpeculationContext rides on every ToolCall. Zero value (Speculative=false,
113	// Epoch=0) means an ordinary committed call, so v0.1 ignores it entirely. A
114	// speculative call (brainstorm 2.6) sets Speculative=true and an Epoch; effects
115	// produced under a non-zero Epoch are PROVISIONAL until Promote/Rollback.
116	type SpeculationContext struct {
117		Speculative bool
118		Epoch       uint64 // speculation epoch id (0 = not speculative)
119		ParentEpoch uint64 // the epoch this one branched from
120	}
121	
122	// TxnID scopes a two-phase context admission (brainstorm 3.4 transactional
123	// context / KV checkpoint-rollback). 0 = auto-commit (no real transaction), so
124	// v0.1 ignores it. A non-zero TxnID means results are admitted to a scratch and
125	// only become visible on Promote(TxnID); Rollback(TxnID) discards them.
126	type TxnID uint64
127	
128	// Outcome is the CLOSED resolution of a provisional call. Default OutcomeCommitted
129	// so a non-speculative, non-transactional call is always "committed".
130	type Outcome uint8
131	
132	const (
133		OutcomeCommitted  Outcome = iota // effects are durable (default)
134		OutcomeSquashed                  // speculative branch discarded; effects retracted
135		OutcomeRolledBack                // transaction rolled back; scratch discarded
136	)
137	
138	// ProvisionalSink is implemented by any driver that produces retractable effects
139	// (the context-MMU is the canonical one). The Kernel drives Promote/Rollback when
140	// a speculation epoch or a transaction resolves. This makes "squash actually
141	// undoes the effect" a frozen cross-driver contract, not a discovered-at-
142	// integration gap. v0.1's MMU registers a sink whose Promote is a no-op append
143	// and whose Rollback drops the scratch.
144	type ProvisionalSink interface {
145		Promote(ctx context.Context, txn TxnID, epoch uint64) error
146		Rollback(ctx context.Context, txn TxnID, epoch uint64) error
147	}
148	
149	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
150	// THE SYSCALL MESSAGE (frozen wire shape; additive-only)
151	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
152	
153	// ToolCall is the request envelope. Op selects an entry in the OPEN operation
154	// table; Tool is the logical tool name (also the lease lane and a training
155	// token). Args is a handle, not bytes. Meta/Ext are OPEN maps — unknown keys MUST
156	// be ignored (forward-compat). Ext carries typed per-subsystem sidecar payloads
157	// keyed by a reserved ExtKey range.
158	type ToolCall struct {
159		Op      OpCode
160		Tool    string
161		Args    Ref
162		Caps    []Capability       // caller-advertised caps (negotiation)
163		Spec    SpeculationContext // SEAM 4 (zero value = ordinary call)
164		Txn     TxnID              // SEAM 4 (0 = auto-commit)
165		SeqNo   uint64             // submission identity (async + speculative)
166		TraceID string
167		Meta    map[string]string // OPEN; unknown keys ignored
168		Ext     map[ExtKey]Ref    // OPEN typed sidecar (spec id, async cursor, label rows...)
169	}
170	
171	// Result is the response envelope. Payload is a handle. Status is the immediate
172	// disposition; Outcome is the provisional-lifecycle resolution. Both are CLOSED
173	// tiny enums.
174	type Result struct {
175		Call    *ToolCall
176		Payload Ref
177		Status  Status
178		Outcome Outcome           // SEAM 4 (default OutcomeCommitted)
179		Meta    map[string]string // OPEN; unknown keys ignored
180		Ext     map[ExtKey]Ref    // OPEN typed sidecar
181	}
182	
183	// Status is the CLOSED immediate disposition. StatusPending is the async seam:
184	// a non-blocking Submit returns Pending and the completion is Reap'd later.
185	type Status uint8
186	
187	const (
188		StatusOK      Status = iota // result is ready in Payload
189		StatusError                 // call failed; Payload may carry an error blob
190		StatusPending               // async: completion will arrive via Reap (SEAM 3)
191	)
192	
193	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
194	// SEAM 1 — VERDICT: a CLOSED trainable union + an OPEN registered range
195	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
196	
197	// VerdictKind is the closed/trainable adjudication vocabulary below
198	// VerdictReservedMax (the set the syscall-tuned small model emits and learns) and
199	// an OPEN registered range above it. A worker that doesn't know a registered kind
200	// resolves it via the kind's FallbackClass (fail-closed by default) and never
201	// panics.
202	type VerdictKind uint16
203	
204	const (
205		VerdictAllow          VerdictKind = iota // the CLOSED trainable set:
206		VerdictDeny                              // a PROVABLE refusal (mirrors decide.go)
207		VerdictTransform                         // rewrite Args (payload: TransformPayload)
208		VerdictQuarantine                        // hold the RESULT out of context (MMU)
209		VerdictRequireWitness                    // gate pending an independent read-back
210		VerdictDefer                             // not adjudicable here; pass to next link
211		// 6..1023 reserved for additive CORE kinds. > VerdictReservedMax: registered.
212		VerdictReservedMax VerdictKind = 1023
213	)
214	
215	// Verdict is a DISCRIMINATED UNION, not a flat struct with "iff" comments: the
216	// Payload is keyed by Kind so a malformed combination (e.g. Kind==Deny with a
217	// transform payload) is UNREPRESENTABLE. This makes every well-formed sample
218	// type-valid by construction — the property that makes Verdict a clean training
219	// target for the syscall-tuned model.
220	type Verdict struct {
221		Kind    VerdictKind
222		Payload VerdictPayload    // nil for Allow/Deny/Defer; typed for the rest
223		Reason  ReasonCode        // CLOSED registered refusal vocabulary (trainable)
224		By      string            // which adjudicator decided (forensics, not dispatch)
225		Meta    map[string]string // OPEN; ignored if unknown
226	}
227	
228	// VerdictPayload is a sealed sum type. Each concrete payload is the typed body of
229	// exactly one Kind. New registered kinds supply their own payload type.
230	type VerdictPayload interface{ isVerdictPayload() }
231	
232	type TransformPayload struct{ NewArgs Ref } // Kind==VerdictTransform
233	func (TransformPayload) isVerdictPayload()  {}
234	
235	type QuarantinePayload struct{ PageOut bool } // Kind==VerdictQuarantine
236	func (QuarantinePayload) isVerdictPayload()   {}
237	
238	type WitnessPayload struct{ Claim string } // Kind==VerdictRequireWitness
239	func (WitnessPayload) isVerdictPayload()   {}
240	
241	// ReasonCode is the CLOSED, registered refusal vocabulary — the model's label
242	// space (mirrors DOS dos_refuse_reasons). Additive-only: a model trained on vN
243	// degrades gracefully on vN+1.
244	type ReasonCode uint16
245	
246	// FallbackClass tells an UNAWARE worker how to treat a verdict kind it doesn't
247	// know. Fail-closed default (FallbackDeny) means an unknown kind can never
248	// silently widen authority.
249	type FallbackClass uint8
250	
251	const (
252		FallbackDeny  FallbackClass = iota // default: unknown kind => deny
253		FallbackAllow                      // explicitly safe-to-ignore kinds
254		FallbackDefer                      // pass to the next link
255	)
256	
257	// ExtKey and OpCode are OPEN registered uint32s with reserved per-subsystem
258	// ranges (see registry.go). Reserved ranges keep KEYS disjoint at link time; the
259	// SEMANTICS of a well-known key are pinned by the typed sidecar that owns it.
260	type (
261		ExtKey uint32
262		OpCode uint32
263	)
264	
265	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
266	// THE FROZEN HOOK INTERFACES (impls live in driver packages, never here)
267	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
268	
269	// Adjudicator is one link in the LSM-style stackable PDP/PEP chain. It returns a
270	// Verdict; the kernel folds the chain by registered Rank (see RegisterVerdictKind
271	// / RegisterAdjudicator). The fold MIRRORS dos-preflake decide.go: a PROVABLE
272	// refusal returns VerdictDeny, an UNPROVABLE one returns VerdictDefer (advisory,
273	// fail-to-abstain). AGT (semantic PDP), the DOS lease PEP, every preflight rung,
274	// and future witness/federated-trust gates all stack here.
275	type Adjudicator interface {
276		Adjudicate(ctx context.Context, c *ToolCall) Verdict
277		Caps() []Capability
278	}
279	
280	// FastPath is the vDSO seam: answer a call LOCALLY with no engine and no remote
281	// round-trip. Miss returns ok=false and the syscall proceeds normally. The 3 v0.1
282	// tiers (pure / content-addressed / static) are registered FastPaths; a new tier
283	// is a new registration.
284	type FastPath interface {
285		Lookup(ctx context.Context, c *ToolCall) (r *Result, ok bool)
286		Caps() []Capability
287	}
288	
289	// Op is one entry in the OPEN operation table (the io_uring opcode analogue). A
290	// new operation (async submit/reap, speculative commit/squash) is a registered
291	// Op, never a new core method. Invoke receives the Kernel so an Op can drive
292	// provisional Promote/Rollback and consult resolver/registries.
293	type Op interface {
294		Code() OpCode
295		Invoke(ctx context.Context, k Kernel, c *ToolCall) (*Result, Verdict)
296	}
297	
298	// Completion is the TYPED async completion (SEAM 3). Reap returns these — the
299	// completion-to-submission binding is a frozen contract, not an opaque cursor, so
300	// two independent async drivers cannot collide on the semantics of a shared key.
301	type Completion struct {
302		Handle SubmissionHandle
303		Result *Result
304	}
305	
306	// SubmissionHandle is the typed identity of a non-blocking Submit.
307	type SubmissionHandle struct {
308		Seq    uint64 // == ToolCall.SeqNo
309		Queue  uint32 // which completion queue (multi-engine / multi-queue)
310		Opaque uint64 // driver-private correlation token
311	}
312	
313	// Emitter observes every lifecycle transition (KPIs, rung labels, the RSI
314	// signal). Stewards, the meta-steward, KPI taps, and the self-labeling harvester
315	// all register here. The label-bearing path is TYPED (Event.Label) so the
316	// model's supervised labels never drift into untyped mush.
317	type Emitter interface{ Emit(ev Event) }
318	
319	type Event struct {
320		Kind    EventKind
321		Call    *ToolCall
322		Verdict *Verdict
323		Result  *Result
324		Label   *LabelRow      // TYPED training signal (nil unless a label-bearing kind)
325		Fields  map[string]any // OPEN; unknown ignored (non-label telemetry only)
326	}
327	
328	type EventKind uint32
329	
330	// LabelRow is the frozen, typed self-labeling signal of the pre-flight ladder
331	// (brainstorm 5.3): "passed cheap rung k, failed expensive rung k+1" is a labeled
332	// hard negative. Freezing the shape means the training-data generator is itself
333	// structured, so the syscall-tuned model's labels can't drift across drivers.
334	type LabelRow struct {
335		CallHash    string
336		RungPassed  int
337		RungFailed  int // -1 if the call passed every rung
338		Verdict     VerdictKind
339		Reason      ReasonCode
340		Speculative bool
341	}
342	
343	// Kernel is the FROZEN chokepoint the whole fleet codes against — the ONLY core
344	// entrypoint. Sync Syscall is defined OVER async Submit/Reap (SEAM 3), so adding
345	// async later never splits the chokepoint: adjudication always happens at Submit.
346	type Kernel interface {
347		// Submit adjudicates (folds the Adjudicator chain) and enqueues the call.
348		// It returns immediately; a synchronous result arrives via Reap. The Verdict
349		// is the admission decision (a denied call is never enqueued).
350		Submit(ctx context.Context, c *ToolCall) (SubmissionHandle, Verdict)
351	
352		// Reap blocks for the completion of a specific submission.
353		Reap(ctx context.Context, h SubmissionHandle) (*Result, error)
354	
355		// Syscall is the synchronous convenience: Submit then Reap. Every existing
356		// caller uses this; async-aware callers use Submit/Reap directly.
357		Syscall(ctx context.Context, c *ToolCall) (*Result, Verdict)
358	
359		// Resolver is the active Ref backend (copy-CAS in v0.1; zero-copy later).
360		Resolver() Resolver
361	
362		// Negotiate intersects a caller's advertised caps with what's registered.
363		Negotiate(advertised []Capability) []Capability
364	}

1	// reasons.go — the CLOSED core refusal vocabulary (the model's label space).
2	//
3	// This is the v0.1 realization of ReasonCode (declared in types.go): a small,
4	// closed, additive set mirroring DOS dos_refuse_reasons. A refusal MUST cite one
5	// of these — never free text — so a deny is verifiable and the deny-loopback can
6	// derive a disposition from the reason's category. Additive-only: a model trained
7	// on this set degrades gracefully when a later minor adds a code.
8	package abi
9	
10	const (
11		ReasonNone           ReasonCode = iota // not a refusal
12		ReasonDefaultDeny                      // no policy affirmatively allowed it (fail-closed)
13		ReasonPolicyBlock                      // an explicit policy rule denied it
14		ReasonSelfModify                       // the call would modify the agent/kernel itself
15		ReasonLeaseHeld                        // a file-tree lease conflict (dos arbitrate)
16		ReasonTrustViolation                   // taint/scope violation (shared-result isolation)
17		ReasonMalformed                        // failed a grammar / arity well-formedness rung
18		ReasonMisroute                         // wrong tool or arg shape — MODEL-FIXABLE
19		ReasonRateLimited                      // throttled; retry after a wait
20		ReasonSecretExfil                      // result/args matched a secret pattern
21		ReasonUnwitnessed                      // require-witness gate had no corroboration
22		ReasonOversize                         // payload exceeded the context-admission budget
23		ReasonUnknownTool                      // tool not in the registry
24		// 14.. reserved for additive core reasons; register out-of-tree names via
25		// RegisterReason.
26		ReasonCoreMax ReasonCode = 1023
27	)
28	
29	var coreReasonNames = map[ReasonCode]string{
30		ReasonNone:           "NONE",
31		ReasonDefaultDeny:    "DEFAULT_DENY",
32		ReasonPolicyBlock:    "POLICY_BLOCK",
33		ReasonSelfModify:     "SELF_MODIFY",
34		ReasonLeaseHeld:      "LEASE_HELD",
35		ReasonTrustViolation: "TRUST_VIOLATION",
36		ReasonMalformed:      "MALFORMED",
37		ReasonMisroute:       "MISROUTE",
38		ReasonRateLimited:    "RATE_LIMITED",
39		ReasonSecretExfil:    "SECRET_EXFIL",
40		ReasonUnwitnessed:    "UNWITNESSED",
41		ReasonOversize:       "OVERSIZE",
42		ReasonUnknownTool:    "UNKNOWN_TOOL",
43	}
44	
45	// ReasonName resolves a reason code to its stable name, consulting the closed
46	// core set first then the registered (out-of-tree) names. Unknown codes render as
47	// REASON_<n> rather than panicking (forward-compat).
48	func ReasonName(c ReasonCode) string {
49		if n, ok := coreReasonNames[c]; ok {
50			return n
51		}
52		reg.mu.Lock()
53		defer reg.mu.Unlock()
54		if n, ok := reg.reasons[c]; ok {
55			return n
56		}
57		return "REASON_" + itoa(uint64(c))
58	}
59	
60	// CoreReasonCount is the size of the closed core vocabulary (excludes NONE) —
61	// referenced by tests asserting the "12-reason" closed set.
62	const CoreReasonCount = 12
63	
64	func itoa(n uint64) string {
65		if n == 0 {
66			return "0"
67		}
68		var b [20]byte
69		i := len(b)
70		for n > 0 {
71			i--
72			b[i] = byte('0' + n%10)
73			n /= 10
74		}
75		return string(b[i:])
76	}
